title: Grad Night

jokes.text: Alexis misinterprets 'I have a favour' as Moira offering to do something for her
jokes.setup: Moira begins 'I have a favour...' intending to ask Alexis for one
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately responds 'Oh, great. Um... I would love a ride to work' — assuming the favour is for her
jokes.explanation: A tight, efficient opening joke that instantly characterizes Alexis as self-absorbed. The misdirection is clean and the punchline lands because 'a ride to work' is such a specific, mundane ask — perfectly calibrated to Alexis's sense of entitlement. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, I was wondering if you might be able to do me a favor—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, yes! Finally! I have been waiting for you to offer me something.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, darling. I'm asking *you* for the favor.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Moira: 'We're all pitching in these days, dear. Like communists or non-union actors.'
jokes.setup: Moira tells Alexis everyone is pitching in and needs to work the motel
jokes.punchline: She equates collective effort with communists OR non-union actors — two wildly different groups united only in Moira's theatrical worldview
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The joke works on multiple levels: the absurd equivalence of communists and non-union actors, Moira's skewed frame of reference where actors are the natural unit of comparison, and the deadpan delivery implied by 'dear.' Only Moira Rose would say this — maximum character integration. The 'or' construction is the key structural flourish: she genuinely can't decide which analogy is more apt, and both are equally ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: She's genuinely confused by normal phrasing because she's self-centered

jokes.text: Alexis reveals her high school graduation is this Friday and she didn't tell her family because she doesn't want them to come
jokes.setup: Alexis asks to be excused from working the motel on Friday
jokes.punchline: She's graduating high school — and deliberately hid it from her family to avoid the embarrassment of them attending
jokes.explanation: Solid character-driven revelation. Alexis hiding her graduation is funny because it inverts the typical family dynamic — she's embarrassed by them, not the other way around. Good situational comedy but the laugh is moderate.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We must all work together as a family. It's the only way we'll survive. We're like communists, except with better bone structure. And non-union actors—willing to do anything for exposure.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'It's bad enough that I have to wear that unflattering black dress and then the hat with the thing on it in front of people that I don't know.'
jokes.setup: Alexis explains why she doesn't want family at graduation
jokes.punchline: She describes a graduation cap as 'the hat with the thing on it' — demonstrating her ignorance of basic educational milestones she's never experienced
jokes.explanation: 'The hat with the thing on it' for a mortarboard is a perfect Alexis line — her obliviousness to normal rites of passage is funny and characterizes her privileged background. The concern about the 'unflattering black dress' placed before the ignorance of the cap name is good structural ordering.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I appreciate the sentiment, but honestly, I don't want you guys there.

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah, I'm not going. I was at your first graduation and it's not my fault that you weren't there.'
jokes.setup: David is told he should attend Alexis's graduation
jokes.punchline: He reveals he attended a previous graduation — implying Alexis was too busy being a wild socialite/absentee student to attend her own first graduation
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent joke. The construction is perfect: the first clause ('I was at your first graduation') creates the expectation of sentimentality, and 'it's not my fault that you weren't there' delivers a devastating, funny gut-punch that reveals volumes about Alexis's chaotic past. It's highly quotable, works better on rewatch, and is fully character-specific. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't want my family at graduation. Do you know what my parents are going to wear? My mother will show up in some kind of caftan situation, and my father will be in a blazer that he's had since 1987. And David... David will probably wear something intentionally unflattering because he thinks it makes a statement. I just can't have that energy at my graduation.

jokes.text: David's birthday excuse: 'It's my birthday.'
jokes.setup: Moira declares everyone must attend Alexis's graduation 'unless you have some special excuse'
jokes.punchline: David, completely deadpan, simply says 'It's my birthday' — delivered with total conviction that this is sufficient
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — three words do the work. The comedy is in the beat: David genuinely believes his birthday is a 'special excuse' not to attend his sister's graduation. The deadpan delivery and structural placement (as the punchline to a scene beat) make it land well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you missed your first graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You were too hungover to walk across the stage at your finishing school in Switzerland.

jokes.text: The family's awkward, unconvincing attempt to claim they 'didn't forget' David's birthday — Moira says they've been 'talking about it for weeks,' David corrects it to 'you've been talking about it for weeks'
jokes.setup: David's birthday is revealed; the family scrambles to claim they hadn't forgotten
jokes.punchline: David quietly corrects 'we've been talking about it' to 'you've been talking about it' — revealing that only one person remembered and it's been mostly talk
jokes.explanation: A subtle, understated cringe beat. The pronoun correction ('we' to 'you') is a small but precise knife. Underplayed well and consistent with the show's warm-but-honest take on family dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My birthday is that day.

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah. I'm very uninterested in that option.' / Alexis: 'As am I.'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira suggest combining the birthday and graduation into one celebration
jokes.punchline: Both siblings reject it with matching, formal disdain — united for once in not wanting to share the spotlight
jokes.explanation: The symmetry of David and Alexis both using formal, adult-sounding rejections ('I'm very uninterested,' 'As am I') is the joke — they're behaving like petulant royalty. Nice doubling effect.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We were just talking about your birthday!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, darling, we've been discussing it all morning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you were the only one talking about it. Dad was reading the newspaper the entire time.

jokes.text: Moira, after David and Alexis both tell the parents not to come to graduation: 'They can't say we didn't try.'
jokes.setup: Both children have firmly rejected any parental presence or shared celebration
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately and cheerfully absolves herself of all responsibility with zero guilt
jokes.explanation: A brief, perfect Moira beat. Her relief at being let off the parenting hook, dressed up as philosophical resignation, is very funny. The line is Moira through and through — self-exculpatory, theatrical, and efficient.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No. Hard pass.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I will not have my birthday conflated with a graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And I'm certainly not sharing my special day with his birthday.

jokes.text: David reacts with 'Ugh' and 'Woof... David' — reaction beat as David presumably sees himself in graduation attire or in a mirror
jokes.setup: Scene transition implies David is seeing himself in the unflattering graduation gown
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Woof... David' — sympathetic but honest — confirms whatever the audience is seeing is not good
jokes.explanation: A visual gag inferrable from context. 'Woof... David' is a great line — the pause before 'David' adds the perfect rhythm of reluctant honesty. The rewatch bonus is for seeing the actual costume.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose that's fine. We'll just... stay home that day.

jokes.text: Patrick asks for a gift receipt, saying it's 'for an anniversary,' and the store clerk immediately launches into a lament about how the world no longer remembers special days
jokes.setup: Patrick asks for a gift receipt for what is clearly a birthday gift
jokes.punchline: The clerk's overly earnest sermon about the world forgetting special days is ironic given what we're about to learn — that David's family HAS forgotten his birthday
jokes.explanation: Works on two levels: the clerk's unsolicited philosophical tangent is funny in itself (small-town eccentric), and it gains retroactive irony given the birthday-forgetting subplot. The rewatch bonus is significant. Moderate impact on first watch.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, what happened to you?

jokes.text: David hints at his family forgetting his birthday via the leading question 'Has your family ever forgotten your birthday? Like your parents and your sister, collectively, as a whole?'
jokes.setup: David is trying to casually reveal his birthday was forgotten without directly saying it
jokes.punchline: The over-specification ('like your parents and your sister, collectively, as a whole') reveals how deeply wounded he is while trying to play it cool
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the escalating specificity — 'collectively, as a whole' is the tell. David is clearly not fine. His attempt to seem casual while being devastatingly specific is excellent character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, a gift receipt. How thoughtful. You know, I once received a gift without a receipt, and it haunted me for years. Much like how my family haunts me for forgetting their birthdays. Well, David's birthday. Just the one. Once.

jokes.text: Patrick describes his family's birthday tradition: sometimes TWO parties — one with friends before school ended, another with cousins who were 'kinda more like siblings'
jokes.setup: Patrick answers David's loaded question about family birthday memory
jokes.punchline: Patrick's description of an idyllic birthday tradition — two parties, close cousins — is hilariously tone-deaf given David is standing there having been completely forgotten
jokes.explanation: Works as secondary cringe — Patrick is obliviously answering the question honestly, making David's situation worse. The 'cousins who were kinda more like siblings' detail is Patrick establishing his warm, normal family in contrast to the Roses.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: If someone's family was terrible, and they had to move to a small town and live in a motel with their parents, would that be... worse than their original situation?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Are you asking hypothetically?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. Hypothetically.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, that's your exact situation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I'm kinda piecing together that it might be your birthday?'
jokes.setup: David has asked increasingly leading questions about birthday forgetting without directly saying anything
jokes.punchline: Patrick, having listened to all of it, gently pieces it together — the understatement of 'kinda piecing together' is the joke
jokes.explanation: Clean, efficient line. The tentative phrasing ('kinda,' 'might be') is endearing and funny — Patrick is gently handling David's obvious deflection. The question mark at the end is the perfect soft landing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Your family ever forget your birthday?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Not only never forgotten, but sometimes I had multiple parties.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would you tell me that?

jokes.text: David: 'Ah, I plan on popping a pill, crying a bit and falling asleep early. So just a regular weeknight.'
jokes.setup: Patrick asks if David has any plans for his birthday
jokes.punchline: David's birthday plan — pill, cry, early sleep — is delivered as utterly mundane, then re-framed as 'just a regular weeknight' to suggest this is his baseline existence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. The list ('popping a pill, crying a bit, falling asleep early') is perfectly calibrated — each item is sadder than the last, but the kicker 'So just a regular weeknight' reframes the devastation as normalcy. That structural move — treating the tragic as mundane — is high-craft. Highly quotable, deeply David, and gets a big laugh because it's both funny and a little heartbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So what are you doing for your birthday?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, you know, the usual. I'll wake up at 4 AM in a cold sweat, mentally catalogue all of my failures, contemplate my own mortality, and then spend the rest of the day wondering if I'm making a huge mistake with my life.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's... that's what you do every day.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know. That's why I'm not doing anything special.

jokes.text: Patrick describes Café Tropical as 'moderately edible' — presenting this as a raving endorsement
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests going to Café Tropical for a birthday dinner, saying he's 'heard people raving about how moderately edible the food is'
jokes.punchline: 'Moderately edible' as the peak of praise for a restaurant — the low bar is the joke
jokes.explanation: Café Tropical is a running gag in the show, so 'moderately edible' lands with fans who know the restaurant's reputation. The phrase itself is perfect — 'raving' + 'moderately edible' is a great comic contrast. Quotable and efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We could go to the cafe downtown. The food is... edible.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Edible? That's your idea of a compliment?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I mean, it's there, and it's open during dinner hours. What more could you want?

jokes.text: 'Those are wet now.' / 'That's a lot of spritzing.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is presumably over-enthusiastically spritzing something at the motel front desk
jokes.punchline: The mundane damage report ('those are wet now') and the equally deadpan observation ('that's a lot of spritzing') from Stevie
jokes.explanation: Inferrable visual gag — Johnny's exuberant motel preparation leads to collateral spritz damage. Stevie's dry delivery is the right register. Moderate laugh, stronger on rewatch when you can see the physical action.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, why are all the plants drowning?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I was just trying to keep them fresh.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fresh? They're not a salad.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't know there was a specific plant watering protocol.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's called not killing them.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Yeah, well, y'know, formal training is usually encouraged.' (responding to Alexis noting applicants went to school 'specifically for vet stuff')
jokes.setup: Alexis is reading impressive vet resumes and noting applicants have actual veterinary education
jokes.punchline: Ted's completely understated 'formal training is usually encouraged' — the 'usually' doing enormous work
jokes.explanation: Ted's gentle sarcasm at his own situation — he hired Alexis without formal training, and 'usually encouraged' is both an understatement and a subtle self-own. The line has good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, the other applicants have experience in municipal administration, they have degrees in public policy...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And I have a degree in, um... being here?

jokes.text: Ted: 'No. God no.'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks if she should be assisting with surgeries
jokes.punchline: Ted's immediate two-word horror response — 'No. God no.' — the 'God' implying he's considered it and the thought is terrifying
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy. The escalation from 'No' to 'God no' in two beats is the entire joke, and it's very well executed. Gets a genuine laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No. No, absolutely not.

jokes.text: Alexis mentions the qualified applicant who went to 'cat camp' dismissively: 'The poor thing went to cat camp.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is arguing that the more qualified applicant (Tiffany Seraris) should be hired
jokes.punchline: She dismisses the qualified applicant by noting she went to 'cat camp' — a credential that Alexis treats as pitiable while simultaneously arguing this person is more qualified than her
jokes.explanation: The contradiction is the comedy: Alexis is genuinely trying to be selfless, but can't help being condescending about cat camp in the same breath. 'The poor thing' is a great touch of Alexis's unconscious snobbery. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, she went to cat camp. I don't think that's a real thing.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Like someone who actually wants to touch animals a lot.'
jokes.setup: Alexis argues there are more qualified people for the vet job
jokes.punchline: She identifies the core qualification as wanting 'to touch animals a lot' — a description so reductive it's accidentally accurate
jokes.explanation: The blunt, reductive phrasing is very Alexis. Her genuine confusion at the concept of vocational calling — distilled to 'wanting to touch animals a lot' — is both funny and revealing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're replacing me?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: With who? Because if it's not someone gorgeous and completely unqualified, then I don't think you're doing this right.

jokes.text: Alexis: '...And some other like gorgeous, slightly under qualified girl is sitting at my desk asking herself the same question.'
jokes.setup: Alexis explains why she should leave so a more qualified person can take her place
jokes.punchline: She imagines her replacement as 'gorgeous, slightly under qualified' — projecting herself onto the hypothetical replacement and revealing she thinks of her main qualification as attractiveness
jokes.explanation: A smart, layered joke. The self-insert ('gorgeous, slightly under qualified') shows Alexis's self-awareness has limits — she understands she's unqualified but genuinely believes gorgeousness is a professional credential. The 'slightly' is perfect understatement.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I don't have any real experience, I've never actually held a job before, and honestly, I don't really know what I'm doing most of the time.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But the main reason I've stuck with it is because Ted makes these terrible puns and I get to laugh at him, and that's basically been my entire job motivation.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Those were genuine laughs?' (re: Alexis saying she came in to laugh at his animal puns)
jokes.setup: Alexis says 'wanting to come in and laugh at your animal puns isn't enough'
jokes.punchline: Ted's hurt/surprised 'Those were genuine laughs?' — he apparently wasn't sure if she was humoring him
jokes.explanation: A sweet little beat. Ted's insecurity about his puns — and his genuine pleasure that she was actually laughing — flips the dynamic and gets a warm laugh. The follow-up 'Sometimes, yeah' from Alexis is the perfect honest qualifier.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Wait, so you were laughing at my puns this whole time?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, some of them were funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Some of them?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, like, one. Maybe one was funny.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Well, I can't say the reception will be as fun.' / 'Like, surprisingly generous.'
jokes.setup: Ted thanks Alexis for her generous decision to step aside
jokes.punchline: 'Surprisingly generous' — the 'surprisingly' does a lot of work, implying Alexis is not typically this selfless
jokes.explanation: Gently teasing without being mean. 'Surprisingly generous' is a soft backhanded compliment that works as both acknowledgment and character commentary.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I have to say, Alexis, it's surprisingly generous of you to quit without notice.

jokes.text: Alexis announces she has 'graduation tonight' at 11am and negotiates to leave at 4 instead of 5
jokes.setup: Alexis just made a genuinely selfless speech about leaving the job
jokes.punchline: She immediately negotiates her exit time to one hour before the workday ends — revealing her selflessness has very practical limits
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency gag. The moment Alexis tries to leave at 11am (it's graduation 'tonight') is funny, then Ted's 'Day ends at five, but sure' is a perfect dry cap.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie! Stevie!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What? What is it? Did the Wi-Fi go out again?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, the Wi-Fi is fine.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Is it the printer?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: The router?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, nothing is broken.

jokes.text: Stevie's instinctive 'Have you tried restarting it?' in response to Johnny calling her name excitedly
jokes.setup: Johnny excitedly calls 'Stevie! Stevie!' presumably with big news
jokes.punchline: Stevie's Pavlovian response assumes it's about the computer again — revealing how often Johnny has had computer problems
jokes.explanation: A small but well-crafted joke. Stevie's assumption reveals the history of their working relationship — Johnny has called her name urgently so many times with tech problems that this is her default response. Strong character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We sold out! Can you believe it? We actually sold out!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the motel had a gas leak, so people needed somewhere to stay for the night.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, I'm stuck on all caps, but no.'
jokes.setup: Johnny deflects Stevie's computer assumption, saying he's not having computer trouble
jokes.punchline: Then immediately admits he IS stuck on all caps — a minor but real tech problem he's apparently just living with
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural move: Johnny denies computer trouble, then immediately reveals minor computer trouble he's accepted as his baseline. The self-correction ('Well, I'm stuck on all caps, but no') is perfectly constructed. The 'but no' holding together despite the all-caps revelation is the heart of the joke. Strong callback to Stevie's assumption.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I want to light up the 'no vacancy' sign.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's broken.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, can we cannibalize parts from the other signs?

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Are you sure you didn't put the same reservation in twice? Because you've done that before.'
jokes.setup: Johnny announces the motel is sold out for the first time
jokes.punchline: Stevie's immediate skepticism — she assumes it's a double-entry error based on precedent
jokes.explanation: Stevie's confident skepticism, backed by specific precedent ('you've done that before'), is a great character beat that also gets a laugh at Johnny's expense. Efficient and well-placed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Come on, Stevie, high-five!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes!

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Oh, yeah, that burnt out years ago. Sadly it hasn't been an issue, but...' (re: the 'No' in the 'No Vacancy' sign)
jokes.setup: Johnny wants to light up the 'No' in the 'No Vacancy' sign
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals it burned out years ago, and 'sadly it hasn't been an issue' — meaning the motel has never been full enough to need it
jokes.explanation: 'Sadly it hasn't been an issue' is a quietly devastating line about the motel's perpetual vacancy. The dark humor is delivered in Stevie's signature deadpan. Strong economy and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: You're doing a solo number? About yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's called 'Moira.'

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Of course it is.

jokes.text: Stevie suggests using the 'No' from the 'No Refunds' sign
jokes.setup: The 'No' in 'No Vacancy' is burned out
jokes.punchline: Stevie's practical suggestion to cannibalize the 'No Refunds' sign — implying the motel also has a 'No Refunds' policy that sees much more regular use
jokes.explanation: A clever extension of the previous joke. The 'No Refunds' sign being the obvious donor implies it gets so much more use than 'No Vacancy.' The pragmatic logic of Stevie's suggestion is funny and consistent with her character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, an ensemble is really just a group of soloists who have agreed to share the same stage.

jokes.text: Johnny tries to get Stevie to do a celebratory high-five / hands in the air moment; Stevie: 'I don't think we're those kind of people.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is ebulliently celebrating the motel being sold out for the first time
jokes.punchline: Stevie flatly refuses the physical celebration: 'I don't think we're those kind of people' — and Johnny continues anyway
jokes.explanation: A perfect distillation of the Johnny/Stevie dynamic. Johnny's unironic enthusiasm against Stevie's institutional world-weariness. 'I don't think we're those kind of people' is a great Stevie line — not mean, just honest. Rewatch bonus for seeing Johnny's physical celebration.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, the good news is... actually, my parents used to do this thing where they'd say 'good news' and then deliver terrible news. Like, 'Good news, we're moving!' and then we'd move to a worse house. So now I have a pavlovian response to the phrase 'good news' where I just feel a sense of dread.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'Oh. Is the bad news that there is no good news? My aunt used to play this game with me a lot.'
jokes.setup: Someone announces they have 'good news and some bad news'
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's immediate fear that the 'good news' is a trick — and her aunt used to do this to her regularly
jokes.explanation: The dark implication that Jocelyn's aunt regularly delivered fake good news is a funny background detail. Her learned defensiveness is the comedy. A small but effective character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, they all came down with this terrible stomach bug. It was absolutely horrific. People were vomiting, they had diarrhea, some of them were doing both simultaneously. There was just... fluids everywhere. One woman said she hadn't left the bathroom in three days. Another man described it as feeling like his insides were trying to become his outsides.

jokes.text: Moira immediately volunteers that she and Johnny won't be attending graduation — before anyone has asked — using Alexis's own browbeating as cover
jokes.setup: Jocelyn announces the Jazzagals have a festival opportunity tonight
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately announces the Roses won't be at graduation — relieved to have a socially acceptable reason to skip it — and cites Alexis's insistence as the reason
jokes.explanation: Moira's premature, enthusiastic abdication of parental duty is consistent with her character. The joke is that she doesn't even wait to be asked — she volunteers the information because she wants it on record. Her framing ('browbeaten us not to attend') places all blame on Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We won't be able to make it to your graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We have a very important commitment that day.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What commitment?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're doing absolutely nothing, and we've already committed to it.

jokes.text: Moira: 'No, Alexis has veritably browbeaten us not to attend. I wouldn't be surprised if she skips the whole affair herself. Waggish little nymph, that she is.'
jokes.setup: Moira explains why the Roses can't attend graduation
jokes.punchline: 'Waggish little nymph, that she is' — Moira's theatrical, almost affectionate way of describing Alexis's behavior, while using elaborate vocabulary to justify her own absence
jokes.explanation: 'Waggish little nymph' is pure Moira — theatrical vocabulary deployed to describe something mundane (her daughter told her not to come). The affection mixed with detachment is very funny and very Moira. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: She's just being a waggish little nymph. You know, she's at that age where she thinks she's discovered irony, and she hasn't quite grasped that the rest of us have been living in it for decades.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'd advise against the mortarboard. It'll flatten the hair and pull focus away from that beautiful face.'
jokes.setup: Moira is helping Alexis prepare for graduation
jokes.punchline: Her advice to not wear the mandatory graduation cap — framed as a compliment about Alexis's face but really just Moira's theatrical instinct for 'look'
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira — giving advice based entirely on appearance/performance rather than convention. 'Pull focus' is an acting term applied to graduation attire, which is very Moira. Warm rather than mean.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, what are you wearing to my graduation?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I was thinking the Valentino, but then I realized I could really make a statement with the graduation cap. I'm having it dyed to match my dress, and I found this woman in town who does the most exquisite beading work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, it's a graduation cap.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Exactly. And it's going to be fierce.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Hm. But by not wearing one, you'll stand out. And by the fact that you're a good ten years older than everyone else.'
jokes.setup: Alexis notes everyone has to wear a mortarboard
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots: not wearing one will make Alexis stand out — then casually adds that being ten years older than her classmates will also make her stand out
jokes.explanation: Strong structure: the first clause sounds like a solution, then the age comment arrives as an afterthought that's somehow worse. The casual delivery of 'a good ten years older than everyone else' as a secondary observation — as if it doesn't matter — is the key. Moira's obliviousness to how this lands is very funny and very true to her character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, darling, you might want to skip the graduation cap. It'll only draw attention to the fact that you're significantly older than your classmates.

jokes.text: Moira: 'You know, it isn't easy. Especially at your age.'
jokes.setup: Moira says she's proud of Alexis for graduating
jokes.punchline: She keeps emphasizing Alexis's age — 'especially at your age' — as Alexis begs her to stop
jokes.explanation: Running gag on Alexis's age extending the previous joke. Alexis's 'Okay, can you please stop saying that' confirms this is a repeated wound being poked. The repetition structure works.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm so proud of you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, thank you, but honestly it was like, super easy. I basically just showed up and it kind of happened.

jokes.text: The entire exchange where Alexis uninvites, then re-invites, then un-invites her mother to the graduation as Moira plays innocent
jokes.setup: Alexis has told her family not to come; now she's suddenly reconsidering
jokes.punchline: As Alexis wavers and extends tentative invitations, Moira plays each card perfectly — 'Well, it's a little late now... Now I'm doing a big concert with the Jazzagals' — punishing Alexis for her original rejection
jokes.explanation: A beautifully sustained bit of cringe comedy. The power dynamic inverts — Alexis now desperately wants Moira there and Moira, having been told not to come, now has the moral high ground. The show understands that both characters are simultaneously right and wrong, and the comedy comes from neither being willing to fully capitulate. 'Now I do. Now I'm doing a big concert with the Jazzagals' is a perfect passive-aggressive line. The warmth underneath makes it genuinely moving as well as funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, I want you to come to my graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course Mommy's coming to your graduation, darling.

jokes.text: David and Patrick's elaborate performance of enthusiasm about Café Tropical's menu — 'Dare we try the deep fried mozzarella stick platter?' / 'They do a lovely wine pairing with that so...'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are on what may be a date at Café Tropical, a famously mediocre restaurant
jokes.punchline: They perform fine-dining enthusiasm for mozzarella sticks with a 'wine pairing' — treating the lowest-tier menu item as a gastronomic adventure
jokes.explanation: Continuing the Café Tropical running gag, now with David and Patrick playing the game together — performing sophistication at a deeply unsophisticated restaurant. Their mutual commitment to the bit is both funny and quietly romantic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You are one of my top priorities.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: One of?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I have a lot on my plate, Alexis. There's my skincare routine, my vocal exercises, my various charitable causes that I'm extremely passionate about but rarely attend.

jokes.text: David's elaborate rationale for wearing a dinner jacket to Café Tropical: he didn't want to wear one of theirs
jokes.setup: Patrick notes David looks very nice
jokes.punchline: David explains he assumed Café Tropical would have a dinner jacket policy and didn't want to wear one of theirs — treating a diner as if it were a Michelin-star restaurant
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific absurdism. David has dressed for a date at Café Tropical as if it's Le Gavroche. The specificity of 'I didn't want to wear one of theirs' — implying his taste is so refined he'd rather bring his own loaner jacket than accept the establishment's — is a great David detail. Also a rewatch bonus given this is their first real date.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, this is wonderful. Nothing says 'successful restaurant opening' like a completely empty dining room.

jokes.text: David: 'In case you didn't know, I'm widely popular. Some might even venture to call me beloved.'
jokes.setup: Stevie arrives at what appears to be a small birthday dinner; someone asks if others are coming
jokes.punchline: David claims he's 'widely popular' and 'beloved' as evidence of why the group is small — the opposite being obviously true
jokes.explanation: Classic David self-deprecation dressed as braggadocio. 'Some might even venture to call me beloved' is an excellent escalation — the formal language ('venture to call') applied to an absurd claim about his own social standing. Quotable and very David.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You look really nice today.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you. I'm wearing Valentino.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I assumed the dress code at Café Tropical was 'black tie or a well-tailored suit.'

jokes.text: Stevie: 'That's a bit of a stretch.'
jokes.setup: David claims some might 'venture to call him beloved'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's immediate flat rejection of the premise
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — four words deflate David's balloon. Stevie's deadpan is consistent with her character. The brevity is the point.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The menu features an extensive selection of proteins, artfully arranged with what appears to be some sort of... vegetable medley. There's also a soup, which I'm told is a bisque, though I suspect it's primarily cream and broken dreams. The desserts are presented in a way that suggests they were either handmade by someone with no formal training, or manufactured sometime in the Reagan administration.

jokes.text: Patrick arrives and the table awkward 'did I not tell you?' exchange — revealing he didn't tell David that Stevie was coming
jokes.setup: Patrick arrives and finds Stevie already there; there's an awkward beat
jokes.punchline: The 'I don't know. Is anyone else coming?' / 'No. This is it.' exchange reveals the miscommunication — and sets up Stevie's date-crashing realization
jokes.explanation: A setup beat for the larger date-crashing joke sequence. Functional cringe comedy — the awkwardness is clear but not yet fully mined.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I think people are intimidated by me. I have a certain... je ne sais quoi. And I think they're also jealous of my success, my looks, my impeccable taste. So really, it's their loss.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'That's a bit of a stretch.' — responding to David calling himself 'beloved'
jokes.setup: David claims some might 'venture to call him beloved'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat 'That's a bit of a stretch' — not unkind, just accurate
jokes.explanation: Stevie's deadpan is reliable but this particular line doesn't have much original content. It's the exact response you'd expect, delivered well. Works because of actor chemistry.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you're telling me that David planned this entire birthday dinner, he's sitting right there staring at you like you hung the moon, and you're worried about whether or not he likes you?

jokes.text: Stevie to David in the bathroom: 'He bought you a present. It's very nicely wrapped. So I think I'm crashing a date?'
jokes.setup: Patrick has stepped away and Stevie uses the opportunity to confer with David
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deductive reasoning — nicely wrapped present = date — delivered with matter-of-fact bluntness
jokes.explanation: The gift-as-date-signal logic is both funny and true. Stevie's cool, investigative tone ('he bought you a present, it's very nicely wrapped') makes the reveal land well. Good setup for the extended bathroom scene.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, so everyone's getting a gift except me? David's getting a gift, Moira's getting a gift, Johnny's getting a gift... I'm the only one without a gift?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God. I'm in a relationship and I'm not getting a gift. That's... that's actually worse than being single.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'He thinks you guys were here, one-on-one. He brought you a present. I didn't even get you a present.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is realizing she's third-wheeling a birthday date
jokes.punchline: Her pivot to guilt about not bringing a present — while crashing a date — is a funny self-aware deflection
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'I crashed a date' to 'I didn't get a present' is a good comic misdirection — Stevie's priorities are hilariously skewed. 'No, I noticed that' from David is the perfect dry cap.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh wow, a scarf. How romantic. Nothing says 'I love you' like something you can strangle yourself with.

jokes.text: David: 'No, I noticed that.'
jokes.setup: Stevie admits she didn't get David a present
jokes.punchline: David's instant, flat confirmation — 'No, I noticed that' — reveals he clocked it immediately but said nothing
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum sting. David's wounded precision — he noticed immediately and stored it — is very funny. The implied petty grievance tracking is pure David.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you put the gift in the closet because... why?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Because that's where I put things when I don't want to deal with them.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's not a system.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's worked so far.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Okay, I'll just say this: if there's anything remotely sentimental in here, he is on a date with you right now.'
jokes.setup: They're debating whether to open Patrick's gift to determine if this is a date
jokes.punchline: Stevie sets up the gift as the litmus test — creating dramatic tension around what Patrick has given
jokes.explanation: Stevie's gift-as-date-detector logic is both funny and sets up the emotional payoff of the store receipt. Great structural setup for the reveal. The pause implied before revealing the gift contents adds to the tension.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I got you something.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. You shouldn't have.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I really think you're going to like it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure it's very thoughtful, but I should tell you that I don't really do gifts. I never have. Growing up, my parents would give me things like a cashmere sweater or a trip to Monaco, and I would just feel this crushing sense of obligation and emptiness inside.

jokes.text: Patrick finds the gift on the table after being opened: 'Oh, I see you found my present.' / 'Um, yeah. We just didn't want anything to spill on it.' / 'So you put it on the table?'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie have opened and examined the gift while Patrick was in the restroom
jokes.punchline: Their cover story ('we didn't want anything to spill on it') makes no sense since they put it ON the table, and Patrick's 'So you put it on the table?' quietly exposes it
jokes.explanation: Patrick's gentle, logical takedown of an obvious lie is perfectly constructed. 'So you put it on the table?' does exactly what it needs to in six words — it's devastating without being cruel. The cringe is light and warm.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, Patrick. This is... this is actually really thoughtful.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, it's not wrapped, and there's no card, but the gesture itself is...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know what? This might be the most meaningful gift anyone's ever given me.

jokes.text: David's reaction to the gift: 'This is the first gift I haven't bought myself in a very long time.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has given David the receipt from their first store sale as a sentimental gift
jokes.punchline: David's revelation that he typically buys his own gifts — the loneliness implied is both funny and touching
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most resonant lines. The comedy and the pathos are inseparable — it's funny that David buys himself gifts, but devastating that this has been the norm. The line reveals David's entire arc: a man who has always provided for himself because no one else did. High character integration, highly quotable, emotionally complex. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: These mozzarella sticks have been sitting under the heat lamp since yesterday.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'You're gonna be really underwhelmed when you open it. Trust me. It's not— See. It's nothing.'
jokes.setup: Everyone urges David to open the gift
jokes.punchline: Patrick's pre-emptive underselling — 'it's nothing' — right before it turns out to be deeply sentimental
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection. Patrick's nervous underselling ('it's nothing') sets up the emotional reveal. On rewatch, knowing what the gift is, 'it's nothing' becomes even funnier. The structure — deny, deny, reveal — is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You know, the food here barely survives being served.

jokes.text: The waitress scrapes off the 'freezer burn' from the mozzarella sticks: 'I overheard someone wanted Mozzarella sticks for their birthday. Pretty sure I scraped off all the freezer burn.'
jokes.setup: Patrick ordered mozzarella sticks to celebrate David's birthday
jokes.punchline: The waitress presents them with the proud caveat that she 'scraped off all the freezer burn' — Café Tropical's version of birthday service
jokes.explanation: A great extension of the Café Tropical running gag. 'Pretty sure I scraped off all the freezer burn' as a point of pride is perfect small-town restaurant comedy. The 'pretty sure' qualifier is the key — she can't confirm all the freezer burn is gone.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, technically, you didn't quit. I let you go. It was a termination.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Much more professional that way.

jokes.text: The waitress's warning: 'I should warn you, these don't travel well. Even coming from the kitchen they lost a lot of shape.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks for the mozzarella sticks to go
jokes.punchline: The waitress warns they 'don't travel well' — even the five-foot trip from the kitchen is too much for these mozzarella sticks
jokes.explanation: The implication that the mozzarella sticks began losing shape before they even left the kitchen is delightfully bleak. 'Even coming from the kitchen they lost a lot of shape' is a perfect Café Tropical line — specific, absurdly sad, deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland? You're my mystery guest?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now hold on, before you say anything, I can explain. I'm not really Roland Schitt. That's just a name I've been using.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've been living under an assumed identity for years. If anyone finds out who I really am, it could destroy everything I've built here.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, you've lived here your whole life. Everyone knows you.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's what they want you to think.

jokes.text: David's awkward frame compliment: 'Um, this is a very solid frame.' / Patrick: 'Thank you, I'm learning.'
jokes.setup: David is clearly moved by the store receipt gift and trying to deflect with a mundane observation
jokes.punchline: David compliments the frame (not the deeply sentimental contents) and Patrick accepts the compliment about the frame specifically — both aware of what's really happening
jokes.explanation: A beautifully underplayed joke that does double duty — it's funny (David deflecting emotion with a frame comment) and it's a sweet character moment (Patrick accepting the deflection with grace). 'Thank you, I'm learning' is a perfect Patrick line — modest, warm, slightly self-deprecating. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I use the name 'Roland Schitt' because people remember it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, people remember the Roses too. We're very well-known in the community.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, but not for good things.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The Rosebud Motel is a respectable establishment!

jokes.text: Ted: 'Let's try to find another word for quit. It kinda implies poor management.'
jokes.setup: Alexis says she 'quit' her job at the vet clinic
jokes.punchline: Ted's reframe — 'quit' implies his management is poor, so they should find another word — revealing his ego is slightly bruised
jokes.explanation: Ted's concern about what 'quit' implies for his management style is a funny self-interested reframe at an emotionally sincere moment. Good structure — the professional concern undercutting the personal one is very Ted.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of doing something special tonight, if you know what I mean.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, you literally just spent twenty minutes explaining your entire evening itinerary.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I'm gonna have to look at those photos before you share them or post them.'
jokes.setup: Ted has taken graduation photos of Alexis
jokes.punchline: Despite the touching reunion moment, Alexis immediately asserts photo approval rights — can't resist brand management even in an emotional scene
jokes.explanation: A perfect character beat. The warm moment is punctuated by Alexis's reflex concern for her image. 'Yeah. Of course. Yeah.' from Ted is the right gentle confirmation.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, I explained it to you already.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, Roland, but I still don't understand what you're saying.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know, but I explained it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I understand that you explained it, but I don't understand the explanation!

jokes.text: Johnny on the phone turning away a family with kids and a grandmother: 'No room at the inn!' / 'I can imagine with that many kids and a grandmother, that it might be tough getting last-minute accommodations but...'
jokes.setup: The motel is sold out; Johnny is turning away guests
jokes.punchline: Johnny's gleeful 'No room at the inn!' — biblical reference deployed with maximum cheerfulness — followed by his total inability to maintain the cold rejection as he empathizes with a family with a grandmother
jokes.explanation: Johnny's warmth defeating his new role as a hard-nosed hotelier is very funny. 'No room at the inn!' is a great choice — triumphant, slightly absurd, very Johnny. His immediate pivot to sympathy ('I can imagine with that many kids...') undercuts his own authority beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't stay for this. I have to go.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, but David, we have a surprise performance!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A surprise performance? Ted, no.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's going to be so much fun! We've been planning it for weeks!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's exactly what I'm afraid of.

jokes.text: Roland reveals he made a reservation under 'Damien Steele' to secretly check into the motel for a grad night role-playing tradition with Jocelyn
jokes.setup: Johnny has been waiting all night for 'Damien Steele,' his last guest, while the full house and big occasion unfolds
jokes.punchline: Damien Steele is Roland — the mayor using a fake name to check into a 'love motel' for a yearly grad night role-play tradition
jokes.explanation: A very well-constructed long-form payoff. 'Damian Steele' was planted earlier and pays off here with maximum absurdity — Roland has an elaborate yearly grad night role-play tradition, uses a fake name, and treats the Schitt's Creek Motel as a love motel. The revelation escalates well and gets a big laugh. Strong structure.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The store is a success.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's really not.

jokes.text: Roland: 'See, Jocelyn and I have a yearly tradition. Every grad night we like to pretend to be other people... if you know what I mean.' / Johnny: 'Yeah, you just told me what you mean.'
jokes.setup: Roland begins to explain his grad night tradition with a conspiratorial wink
jokes.punchline: Johnny cuts off the 'wink wink' with 'Yeah, you just told me what you mean' — refusing to play the game of pretending Roland was subtle
jokes.explanation: A great Johnny/Roland dynamic joke. Roland's 'if you know what I mean' implies mystery; Johnny's flat correction destroys the mystique. The joke works because Johnny is right — Roland did tell him exactly what he meant. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I should probably tell you something. I'm not really experienced with this kind of thing.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, I've been with women. Several women. But I've never... I've never felt this way before. With anyone.

jokes.text: Roland explains the tradition: 'The mayor of the town can't use his real name to check into a love motel.' Johnny: 'Yeah, it's not a love motel, Roland.'
jokes.setup: Roland explains the need for an alias at the motel
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat correction — 'it's not a love motel' — delivered with genuine dignity as a business owner who does not want this designation
jokes.explanation: Johnny's indignant correction is one of his best character beats in the scene. His investment in the motel's dignity vs. Roland's casual 'love motel' designation is a nice ongoing tension.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, that's what I do. I create moments. I'm a moment creator.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Oh, we've been doing this ever since our own grad night. If you catch my drift.' / Johnny: 'Yeah, I caught your drift when you told me what you mean.'
jokes.setup: Roland adds further context with another winking phrase
jokes.punchline: Johnny's callback to his previous line — 'I caught your drift when you told me what you mean' — extending the joke with perfect structural symmetry
jokes.explanation: The callback to joke #61 with a slight variation ('caught your drift when you told me what you mean') is extremely well constructed. The joke gets funnier with the repetition because Johnny's exasperation escalates. This is the kind of escalating callback structure that distinguishes good comedy writing from mediocre. The line lands harder the second time.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We can talk whenever you want.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, not in the mornings. I'm not a morning person.

jokes.text: The grad night performance reveal — the Jazzagals perform 'Baby I'm Yours' for the graduating class
jokes.setup: An announcer promises an 'extra special surprise' for graduates
jokes.punchline: The Jazzagals — a small-town choir led by Moira Rose — begin performing a somewhat overwrought version of 'Baby I'm Yours' as the grad night entertainment
jokes.explanation: The irony that Moira skipped Alexis's graduation to perform at... Alexis's graduation (the same venue) is the central irony of the episode. The performance itself is a visual comedy moment — Moira Rose performing with a small-town choir is inherently funny. Rewatch bonus for the visual of the performance.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose this is what we get for our parenting.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? We're excellent parents.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, the cake says 'Happy Birthday Alexis and David.' Our children's names are Alexis and David.

jokes.text: Ted hesitates to leave when the Jazzagals start — 'Oh really? She said extra special. I think we should stay.' Alexis has to drag him out.
jokes.setup: Alexis and Ted are trying to slip out of the graduation; the Jazzagals start performing
jokes.punchline: Ted is genuinely excited by the promise of 'extra special' entertainment and wants to stay — reading the situation completely backwards from Alexis
jokes.explanation: A small but effective character beat — Ted's earnest, wholesome enthusiasm is at odds with Alexis's urgency. Mild but pleasant.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I appreciate the gesture, but I would have preferred if my birthday and Alexis's graduation were celebrated on separate occasions. It's difficult enough to be the center of attention without having to share it.

jokes.text: David: 'I'm really glad you decided to invest in your business, David.' / Patrick: '...And I'm so glad you did, Patrick, because you've really helped to turn it into the success that it is.' / Patrick: 'Hmm. A bold claim.'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are in a warm post-dinner conversation
jokes.punchline: Patrick ventriloquizes David's voice to say something complimentary about Patrick, then immediately undercuts it with 'A bold claim' — self-deprecating about the store's success
jokes.explanation: A charming, meta comedic exchange where Patrick essentially tells David what he should say, then pokes fun at the claim. 'A bold claim' is a perfect Patrick line — self-aware, modest, warm. The constructed dialogue (Patrick voicing both sides) is a nice structural touch.

jokes.text: David: 'Um, I've never done that before... with a guy.'
jokes.setup: After what appears to be their first kiss, David thanks Patrick with an earnest statement
jokes.punchline: The pause before 'with a guy' reframes the statement — David has kissed people before but not men, and this is a significant first
jokes.explanation: A pivotal moment that is both comedic and deeply significant. The pause before 'with a guy' is structurally elegant — it sets up the expectation of awkward inexperience ('never done that before') and then specifies it's gender-specific, not experience-specific. The comedy and the emotional weight are perfectly balanced. High character integration — this is a David Rose moment through and through.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I was getting a little scared that I was gonna let you leave here without us having done that.'
jokes.setup: Patrick responds to David's first-kiss revelation
jokes.punchline: Patrick's understated admission that he was scared he'd miss his chance — delivered with calm relief rather than drama
jokes.explanation: Patrick's quiet bravery — admitting he was scared without melodrama — is very sweet and slightly funny. The understatement ('a little scared') applied to a significant emotional moment is perfectly Patrick. The warmth of this scene earns the character comedy designation.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, um, fortunately, I'm a very generous person.'
jokes.setup: Patrick thanks David for 'making that happen for us'
jokes.punchline: David deflects the emotional weight by characterizing kissing Patrick as an act of personal generosity on his own part
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect David Rose line. He can't accept straightforward romantic acknowledgment without armoring it in irony, so he frames his emotional availability as a charitable act. 'Fortunately, I'm a very generous person' is simultaneously defensive, funny, and secretly tender. High character integration. Highly quotable. The 'fortunately' is the key word — as if Patrick has been lucky to encounter David's generosity.

jokes.text: David: 'Just preferably not before 10 a.m., 'cause I'm not really a morning person.'
jokes.setup: Patrick says they can talk 'whenever you'd like'
jokes.punchline: David immediately sets a condition on his emotional availability: not before 10am
jokes.explanation: David's pivot from romantic to logistical is perfect character comedy. The very specific '10am' caveat — immediately after their first kiss — deflates the romance just enough to be funny without undermining it. 'Cause I'm not really a morning person' is an endearingly mundane reason.

jokes.text: 'Happy birthday, David.' / 'Happy graduation, Alexis.' — siblings sharing a quiet moment
jokes.setup: The episode is winding down; siblings are outside together
jokes.punchline: The formal exchange of acknowledgments — the same words used in a toast, but stripped of ceremony — is both sweet and slightly funny in its mutual awkwardness
jokes.explanation: A warm, clean moment. The mirrored structure of the exchange is the comedy — neither sibling knows how to be fully sincere so they stick to official language. Understated and effective.

jokes.text: The family attempts 'For He's a Jolly Good Fellow' — with escalating pronoun chaos: 'For she's a jolly good fellow' / 'For they're two jolly good fellows' / 'For graduating today AND being born today'
jokes.setup: The family tries to sing 'For He's a Jolly Good Fellow' for the combined birthday/graduation
jokes.punchline: The song immediately falls apart as they can't agree on pronouns, then gender, then give up entirely with an improvised lyric about 'graduating today and being born today'
jokes.explanation: The combined celebration that both David and Alexis rejected at the start of the episode is exactly what they get — and it's a mess. The escalating pronoun chaos ('he/she/they') resolved by the completely improvised 'for graduating today and being born today' is very funny. The structural callback to the opening is satisfying.

jokes.text: The birthday cake reads 'Happy day Alex and Davis'
jokes.setup: A birthday/graduation cake is revealed
jokes.punchline: The cake reads 'Happy day Alex and Davis' — wrong names, wrong occasion — and Moira offers: 'We've done the best we can'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The names are wrong (Alex, Davis instead of Alexis, David), the occasion is wrong ('Happy day'), and Moira's serene 'We've done the best we can' treats this as a triumph. It's the perfect encapsulation of the Rose family's loving dysfunction. Highly quotable, great visual gag, strong callback to the forgotten-birthday thread. The gap between 'we've been talking about it for weeks' and 'Happy day Alex and Davis' is devastating and perfect.

jokes.text: Roland interrupting the family toast to note Johnny sold out the motel 'for the first time ever'
jokes.setup: The family is celebrating David and Alexis's achievements
jokes.punchline: Roland hijacks the moment to credit Johnny's motel success — framing selling out a very modest motel as equivalent to the personal milestones being celebrated
jokes.explanation: Roland's oblivious redirect of the family moment to motel news is consistent with his character. The callback to the sold-out motel subplot wraps the episode nicely, though the joke itself is lighter.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, I think this is really pulling focus from my birthday a bit though.'
jokes.setup: The family is singing an inclusive version of the birthday song covering everyone
jokes.punchline: David objects that the collective celebration is 'pulling focus' from his birthday specifically — his self-absorption winning out even during a warm family moment
jokes.explanation: A great closing joke. 'Pulling focus' is a perfect word choice — theatrical, specific, very David. His complaint that a family celebration is 'pulling focus from his birthday' while simultaneously being the birthday boy is maximally self-absorbed and maximally funny. Strong character integration and a great episode button.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Stevie celebrate having a fully booked hotel; David and Patrick go on a date.
insight: Deadpan understatement drives a character-heavy hour with modest 75-point ceiling.
